concepts.title: Storage Architecture: Log Layer vs KV Layer
concepts.content:
Key ideas:
- Log layer: immutable, append-only file storage — content-addressed by rootHash
- KV layer: mutable key-value store built on top of Log layer — uses stream IDs
- Erasure coding: data is split and redundantly encoded across multiple nodes
- Data sharding: files are divided into sectors and distributed for parallel retrieval

0G Storage has two distinct layers, each optimized for different access patterns.

**Log Layer (Immutable):**
- Store any file (text, binary, model weights, datasets)
- Content-addressed: identified by rootHash (Merkle root)
- Immutable: once stored, data never changes
- Use cases: datasets, model weights, documents, images, audit records

**KV Layer (Mutable):**
- Key-value pairs grouped by stream ID
- Mutable: values can be updated, keys can be overwritten
- Built on top of the Log layer (KV writes become Log entries)
- Use cases: AI agent state, configuration, user profiles, session data

**When to use which:**

| Use Case | Layer | Why |
|----------|-------|-----|
| Upload a training dataset | Log | Immutable, content-addressed |
| Store model weights | Log | Large binary, immutable once trained |
| AI agent conversation history | KV | Updates frequently |
| Application configuration | KV | Needs to be mutable |
| Audit trail records | Log | Must be tamper-proof |
| User session state | KV | Changes per interaction |

**Under the hood:**
Both layers use erasure coding — data is encoded with redundancy so that any k-of-n storage nodes can reconstruct the original. This means your data survives even if some nodes go offline. Data is also sharded across multiple nodes for parallel upload and download, achieving ~200 MBPS retrieval speed.

quiz.question: What's the best practice for managing rootHashes in a production 0G application?
options: Keep them only in application memory — they're easy to recompute
Save to a local file only — blockchain storage is too expensive
Save to both a local file and on-chain (smart contract) for redundancy
quiz.correctAnswer: Save to both a local file and on-chain (smart contract) for redundancy
